## Site Navigation

## You are here:

# Fire-safety Engineering Delivers Lifesaving Value

## Article Details

![](https://ventures.jhu.edu/wp-content/themes/jhtv/images/placeholder.png)
![](https://ventures.jhu.edu/wp-content/uploads/2025/05/thomas-gernay-team_JHU0641-300x200.jpg)

*The following was originally published on [The Hub](https://hub.jhu.edu/2025/05/06/nsf-funding-thomas-gernay-fire-resistant-buildings/).* 

*A computational tool developed by Johns Hopkins civil engineer Thomas Gernay predicts how fire affects structures, leading to more resilient designs and enhanced public safety*

As wildfires threaten densely populated areas worldwide with increasing frequency and intensity, [Thomas Gernay](https://engineering.jhu.edu/faculty/thomas-gernay/), an associate professor in civil and systems engineering at Johns Hopkins University, is relying on funding from the National Science Foundation and National Institute of Standards and Technology to develop new designs for fire-resistant buildings and bridges.

Gernay’s approaches not only make structures safer but also more economical, enabling efficient use of materials while enhancing building codes, improving structural resilience, and providing engineers with the tools they need to design safer structures overall.

“Our work sits at the intersection of fire safety and structural engineering, focusing on creating scalable solutions to make materials, structures, and even entire infrastructure systems more fire resilient,” said Gernay, director of Johns Hopkins’ [Multi-Hazard Resilient Structures lab](https://mars.jhu.edu/) and a member of the [Hopkins Extreme Materials Institute](https://hemi.jhu.edu/). “By rigorously modeling how extreme heat affects materials and developing new design approaches, we aim to transform how buildings—and even whole cities—can withstand and recover from fire events.”

Gernay is the co-developer of [SAFIR](https://engineering.jhu.edu/safir/), a computational tool that allows researchers and designers to simulate how structures respond to fire. Today, SAFIR is used by more than 300 institutions across 43 countries, playing a key role in projects as varied as the design of the Paris 2024 Olympics Aquatic Center and forensic analyses of the 2023 I-95 Philadelphia bridge collapse caused by a tanker truck fire. Having this technology allows engineers to better predict how fire affects structures, leading to more resilient designs and enhanced public safety.

His team’s research on fire design methods for building structures using cold-formed steel (CFS) is also having a profound impact on the industry. Working with the American Iron and Steel Institute, Gernay and several collaborators, including [Whiting School of Engineering](https://engineering.jhu.edu/) colleague [Benjamin Schafer](https://engineering.jhu.edu/faculty/benjamin-schafer/), identified shortcomings in current fire design approaches—as well as opportunities for the industry. This work led to a new fire appendix in the 2024 American National Standards Institute’s design specifications for CFS structural members—a crucial resource for designing fire-resistant buildings.

Supported by a five-year, $649,000 National Science Foundation Early CAREER Award, Gernay is expanding on that work, refining performance-based fire design methods that will allow engineers to model fire resilience with even greater accuracy.

“The total economic loss from the 2025 Los Angeles fires is expected to be close to $250 billion. Even when fires do not occur, they are still costly due to current protection measures,” Gernay said. “For every dollar invested in a steel frame structure, we may pay up to 20 cents for fire protection, like adding fire-resistant material to beams and columns, and at the scale of the nation, this fire protection amounts to tens of billions of dollars.”

He said that the motivation for his group’s research “is not only better designs, which can save lives, but also designs that are smarter and more efficient to reduce the economic impact of fire—delivering value for the public through better engineering.”

While Gernay’s NSF-funded project focuses on performance-based fire design methodologies, his interest in the economic aspects of fire safety extends to other projects as well, including one supported by the National Institute of Standards and Technology (NIST) to study the economic impact of advanced fire designs for steel-concrete buildings in the U.S. The project assesses construction costs, lifetime costs, and its effect on the speed of construction. Gernay will also analyze case studies and conduct probabilistic numerical modeling to evaluate the fire performance and damage costs for various fire scenarios.

“This study supports new approaches to fire design in building codes for more efficient use of resources in the U.S. construction industry,” Gernay said, pointing out that the approach of assessing the cost and benefits of safety investments can apply to any hazard—providing a science-based method to ensure resources are achieving optimal impacts.

“Fire is an unavoidable threat, but through research, we can minimize its risks and provide greater life safety,” he said. “Without support from organizations like the NSF and NIST, advancements in fire safety would be slower, leaving structures and communities more vulnerable. Science and engineering are the difference between destruction and safety. Scientific discovery is what allows us to have more resilient, performant, and affordable buildings.”

*JHTV supports Gernay’s SAFIR work.*

# ENGINEERING QUALITY CHECKS : PEER REVIEW IN FIRE SAFETY ENGINEERING

###### [Uncategorized](https://scspng.com/category/uncategorized/)

![](https://scspng.com/wp-content/uploads/2024/06/SCSP-OP-TSM-FM-PRIFSE-PEER-REVIEW-IN-FIRE-SAFETY-ENGINEERING-2024-A01-1.png)

Peer review plays a pivotal role in ensuring rigorous standards and advancing innovation in fire safety engineering. This collaborative process involves experts from academia, industry, and regulatory bodies critically evaluating research, methodologies, and practical applications within the field. In fire safety engineering, peer review begins with the submission of research papers, proposals, or engineering designs for evaluation by peers who possess specialized knowledge and experience. These reviewers assess the technical soundness, accuracy of calculations, appropriateness of methodologies, and adherence to relevant codes and standards such as NFPA (National Fire Protection Association) and ISO (International Organization for Standardization).

Peer review in fire safety engineering fosters excellence by promoting transparency, rigor, and reliability in research and practice. For instance, in the development of new fire suppression systems, peer reviewers scrutinize experimental data, simulation models, and performance criteria to ensure that proposed systems meet safety requirements and effectively mitigate fire risks. Case studies from the construction industry demonstrate the impact of rigorous peer review. Following the Grenfell Tower fire tragedy in London, peer-reviewed investigations identified deficiencies in building materials and fire safety strategies, leading to comprehensive reforms in fire safety regulations and building codes across the UK and beyond.

Moreover, peer review serves as a cornerstone for continuous improvement and adaptation to emerging challenges in fire safety engineering. For example, advancements in materials science and digital simulation technologies have prompted peer-reviewed studies on the fire-resistant properties of new building materials and the effectiveness of computer-aided modeling in predicting fire behavior. These reviews validate innovations and ensure that new technologies uphold safety standards while offering potential advancements in fire safety strategies.

Looking ahead, the role of peer review in fire safety engineering is set to expand with the integration of data-driven approaches and interdisciplinary collaborations. Real-time monitoring systems, IoT sensors, and predictive analytics are transforming fire safety practices, offering opportunities for peer-reviewed research to validate the reliability and effectiveness of these technologies. By leveraging peer review to validate research findings, exchange knowledge, and uphold standards, fire safety engineers can continue to innovate, enhance safety protocols, and mitigate fire risks effectively in diverse environments and applications.

Conclusively, peer review in fire safety engineering not only upholds rigorous standards but also drives continuous improvement and innovation. Through meticulous evaluation of research and practical applications, peer reviewers ensure that fire safety strategies and technologies meet the highest levels of reliability, effectiveness, and compliance with regulatory requirements. As technologies evolve and challenges grow, the role of peer review remains indispensable in safeguarding lives, protecting property, and advancing the field of fire safety engineering globally.

Read more [INFRASTRUCTURE FIRE ENGINEERING](https://scspng.com/4-0-structural-fire-engineering/) and [FIRE SAFETY ENGINEERING IN NIGERIA AND THE STRATEGIES FOR RISK MITIGATION](https://scspng.com/fire-safety-engineering-in-nigeria-and-the-strategies-for-risk-mitigation/)

--- SOURCE 4: Coupled hybrid modelling in fire safety engineering; a literature review ---
URL: https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0379711217303028

SUMMARY:
Coupled hybrid modelling in fire safety engineering; a literature review - ScienceDirect The field of building simulation (the study of ventilation and air quality in buildings) use coupled hybrid modelling – these instances involve the coupling of a “multizone model” (a 1D network model) and a field model [19,( ( ( ( ( ( ( In building simulation, the field sub-model is typically used to simulate external wind conditions around the building and not features inside the building. Wildland fire researchers use coupled hybrid methods, typically called atmosphere-fire coupling, to examine the interaction of wildfire and atmospheric systems [23,( ( ( Studies couple a field model (used to simulate mass and enthalpy flow in the atmosphere above a wildfire) and an empirical 2D fire spread model.

Coupled hybrid modelling in fire safety engineering; a literature review
========================================================================

Author links open overlay panel Benjamin Ralph, Ricky Carvel

Show more

Outline

Add to Mendeley

Share

Cite

[https://doi.org/10.1016/j.firesaf.2018.08.008](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.firesaf.2018.08.008 "Persistent link using digital object identifier")[Get rights and content](https://s100.copyright.com/AppDispatchServlet?publisherName=ELS&contentID=S0379711217303028&orderBeanReset=true)

Under a Creative Commons [license](http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc-nd/4.0/)

Open access

Highlights
----------

*   •Coupled hybrid modelling can reduce wall clock time for total system analysis. 
*   •Work is being wasted due to lack of collaboration. 
*   •There is no consensus on many technical aspects of coupled hybrid modelling. 

Abstract
--------

Systems in the built environment are getting bigger and more complex. Fire safety engineers are required to analyse these structures to ensure acceptable levels of safety. Computational limitations mean that the calculation domain must be curtailed. This ignores the two-way coupling between the total system and a fire. Coupled hybrid modelling (coupling of fire dynamics sub-models with a range of computational costs) expands the domain and analyses this two-way coupling within a reasonable timeframe. This article presents a literature review of this modelling paradigm and has application for those investigating and expanding the method.

Over the last quarter of a century, researchers have investigated coupled hybrid modelling but work has been in disconnected streams. There has been no review of coupled hybrid modelling for fire safety engineering. It is unclear where the knowledge gaps are and where future work should be focused.

This review demonstrates that the method is numerically feasible and can reduce wall clock time for total system analysis. This review reveals that there is limited validation and a host of unresolved questions (including sub-model choice, interface modelling, [domain decomposition](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/domain-decomposition "Learn more about domain decomposition from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") and coupling method). This review draws attention to the lack of collaboration which has led to obsolete models and parallel working.

This article shows that coupled hybrid modelling has potential but effort is being squandered. This review is a stepping-stone towards a standardised coupled hybrid framework. This review highlights where future collaborative research should be directed.

*   [Previous article in issue](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0379711217307154)
*   [Next article in issue](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0379711217307531)

Keywords
--------

Coupled modelling

Hybrid modelling

Multiscale modelling

CFD

Field modelling

Zone modelling

Network modelling

Fire safety engineering

1. Introduction
---------------

### 1.1. Problem

The construction industry is driven by time constraints [[1](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0379711217303028#bib1)] and these constraints can lead to compromises in engineered safety [[2](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0379711217303028#bib2)]. As with other fields, this is true for fire safety engineers, designers and modellers. To deliver output within reasonable and expected timeframes, modellers curtail the domain to keep simulation runtimes low [[3](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0379711217303028#bib3),[4](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0379711217303028#bib4)]; as shown in [Fig. 1](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0379711217303028#fig1). Modellers explicitly consider a small part of a total system (e.g. a single room in a building or a short section of a tunnel) and expand conclusions to the entire system [[5](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0379711217303028#bib5)].

![Image 11: Fig. 1](https://ars.els-cdn.com/content/image/1-s2.0-S0379711217303028-egi10G8R2GP4RG.jpg)
1.   [Download: Download high-res image (293KB)](https://ars.els-cdn.com/content/image/1-s2.0-S0379711217303028-egi10G8R2GP4RG_lrg.jpg "Download high-res image (293KB)")
2.   [Download: Download full-size image](https://ars.els-cdn.com/content/image/1-s2.0-S0379711217303028-egi10G8R2GP4RG.jpg "Download full-size image")

Fig. 1. Typical fire safety engineering modelling paradigm.

Over half of fire fatalities in the built environment in the US, UK and Australia occur outside of the room of fire origin [[[6]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0379711217303028#bib6), [[7]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0379711217303028#bib7), [[8]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0379711217303028#bib8)]. Over 65% of UK fire fatalities are due to smoke inhalation [[9](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0379711217303028#bib9)]. The entire building system and its ventilation have significant influence on how fire behaves and how smoke is generated and spreads throughout system [[10](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0379711217303028#bib10)]. The current typical fire safety engineering modelling paradigm ignores this two-way interaction of the total system and the fire. The acuteness of this risk is increasing as buildings are getting taller [[11](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0379711217303028#bib11)] and more complex [[12](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0379711217303028#bib12)], tunnels are getting longer [[13](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0379711217303028#bib13)], and the whole built environment is becoming more reliant upon performance based design [[14](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0379711217303028#bib14)].

One of the solutions to address this issue is “coupled hybrid” modelling – coupling multiple sub-models, with the same function but with differing complexities and computational costs, into a single simulation tool (refer to [Fig. 2](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0379711217303028#fig2)). This coupled hybrid method enables modellers to expand the calculation domain and explicitly examine more, or all, of a total system [[15](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0379711217303028#bib15),[16](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0379711217303028#bib16)].

![Image 12: Fig. 2](https://ars.els-cdn.com/content/image/1-s2.0-S0379711217303028-egi10Q3VG2MRTP.jpg)
1.   [Download: Download high-res image (196KB)](https://ars.els-cdn.com/content/image/1-s2.0-S0379711217303028-egi10Q3VG2MRTP_lrg.jpg "Download high-res image (196KB)")
2.   [Download: Download full-size image](https://ars.els-cdn.com/content/image/1-s2.0-S0379711217303028-egi10Q3VG2MRTP.jpg "Download full-size image")

Fig. 2. Schematic of coupled hybrid modelling for tunnels and buildings.

#### 1.1.1. What's in a name?

Coupled fluid modelling has been investigated in a wide range of fields of study (haemodynamics [[17](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0379711217303028#bib17)], [indoor air](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/indoor-air "Learn more about indoor air from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") quality [[18](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0379711217303028#bib18)], building ventilation [[19](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0379711217303028#bib19)], including fire [[20](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0379711217303028#bib20)], tunnel ventilation [[21](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0379711217303028#bib21)], including fire [[22](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0379711217303028#bib22)], wildland fire [[23](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0379711217303028#bib23)] and climatology [[24](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0379711217303028#bib24)]). Each field has slightly differing terminology for this method; including coupled, hybrid, integrated, multiscale, two-scale, multi-dimensional, 3D-1D, field-zone, field-network and others. In this review, we adopt the catch all term “coupled hybrid” to describe the coupling of two or more sub-models (which have the same overarching function) into a single hybrid model. It is acknowledged that some coupled hybrid models may also be multiscale (work at multiple scales of time and space), 3D-1D (couple 3D and 1D fluid solvers), field-zone (couple a field model and a zone model), etc.

#### 1.1.2. Modelling methods for fire and smoke

Here we present a short description of the model types discussed in this review (refer to [Fig. 3](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0379711217303028#fig3) for an illustration of the model types). The definitions are not designed to be comprehensive explanations but instead to give a broad overview and to point those interested to further reading. The models are presented in order of increasing complexity and computational cost.

![Image 13: Fig. 3](https://ars.els-cdn.com/content/image/1-s2.0-S0379711217303028-egi10PVXNNR9C9.jpg)
1.   [Download: Download high-res image (258KB)](https://ars.els-cdn.com/content/image/1-s2.0-S0379711217303028-egi10PVXNNR9C9_lrg.jpg "Download high-res image (258KB)")
2.   [Download: Download full-size image](https://ars.els-cdn.com/content/image/1-s2.0-S0379711217303028-egi10PVXNNR9C9.jpg "Download full-size image")

Fig. 3. Hierarchy of typical fire safety engineering simulation methods.

1D network models represent a system as a one-dimensional network of nodes (compartments or junctions) and node connections (ducts, tunnels, corridors or leakage paths). Nodes contain a single set of variables such as temperature, density, mass and are treated as homogeneous. Node connections represent 1D transfer conduits between nodes. Network models contain relatively simple forms of conservation equations such as the use of [Bernoulli's equation](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/earth-and-planetary-sciences/bernoulli-theorem "Learn more about Bernoulli's equation from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") for the conservation of momentum and hence enable a large domain to be analysed with low computational cost [[25](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0379711217303028#bib25)]. Examples of network models include the [Subway](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/subways "Learn more about Subway from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") Environmental Simulator (SES) [[26](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0379711217303028#bib26)] and Fire and Smoke Simulator (FSSIM) [[25](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0379711217303028#bib25)].

Zone models represent a compartment as multiple uniform zones (typically two zones: a hot upper layer and a cooler lower layer) with the inclusion of vents to represent doors and windows [[27](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0379711217303028#bib27)]. Zone models solve conservation equations between the uniform zones and typically include empirical relationships for phenomena such as fires, plume flow and corridor jets. Zone models are limited by the geometry they can represent (simple, cuboidal compartments) but are solved relatively quickly. Examples of zone models include CFAST [[28](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0379711217303028#bib28)] and BRANZFIRE [[29](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0379711217303028#bib29)].

Field models, also called [computational fluid dynamics](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/computational-fluid-dynamics "Learn more about computational fluid dynamics from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") (CFD) models, divide a domain into [finite elements](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/finite-element-method "Learn more about finite elements from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") or volumes for which conservation equations are solved. Each finite element holds a set of conserved variables. Field models can be used to examine complex geometry but require large storage space, high computation requirements and have a high computational cost. Due to typical meshing strategies, field models are not well suited to studying leakage through small gaps in a relatively large enclosure [[30](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0379711217303028#bib30)]. Examples of field models include FDS [[31](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0379711217303028#bib31)], SMARTFIRE [[32](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0379711217303028#bib32)] and FireFOAM [[33](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0379711217303028#bib33)].

The term “coupled modelling” is sometimes also used in fire science to describe the coupling of field models and solid-phase heat transfer and structural response models [[34](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0379711217303028#bib34),[35](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0379711217303028#bib35)]. These are not considered to be “hybrid” models (the sub-models do not perform essentially the same function) and are outside of the scope of this review.

2. Coupled hybrid modelling in other fields
-------------------------------------------

The haemodynamics industry have employed coupled 3D-1D hybrid fluid models to simulate multiscale blood flow through vessels [[17](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0379711217303028#bib17),[[36]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0379711217303028#bib36), [[37]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0379711217303028#bib37), [[38]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0379711217303028#bib38), [[39]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0379711217303028#bib39), [[40]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0379711217303028#bib40), [[41]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0379711217303028#bib41)]. Coupled hybrid modelling in haemodynamics also incorporates unsteady geometric deformation of the vessel; typically via the use of FEM [[42](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0379711217303028#bib42)].

The automotive industry use coupled 3D-1D hybrid fluid and combustion models to simulate [internal combustion engines](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/earth-and-planetary-sciences/internal-combustion-engine "Learn more about internal combustion engines from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") [[[43]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0379711217303028#bib43), [[44]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0379711217303028#bib44), [[45]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0379711217303028#bib45), [[46]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0379711217303028#bib46)]. Coupled hybrid methods enable the entire system, including [combustion chamber](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/earth-and-planetary-sciences/combustion-chamber "Learn more about combustion chamber from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), fuel injection, exhaust, intake and filters to be efficiently modelled. The method is used especially during engine development stage. [1D models](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/1d-model "Learn more about 1D models from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") typically used to simulate whole engine behaviour are phenomenological and require fitting to experimental data. To address the lack of validation data, 3D fluid models are used to capture complex [combustion processes](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/combustion "Learn more about combustion processes from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") and pollutant generation [[47](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0379711217303028#bib47)].

Tunnel ventilation researchers and practitioners have developed and used coupled 3D-1D hybrid models for the “multi-dimension” design and assessment of [ventilation systems](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/ventilation-system "Learn more about ventilation systems from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") and passenger comfort and safety [[21](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0379711217303028#bib21),[[48]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0379711217303028#bib48), [[49]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0379711217303028#bib49), [[50]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0379711217303028#bib50)]. In this industry the use of 1D network models to design ventilation systems is typical [[26](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0379711217303028#bib26),[51](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0379711217303028#bib51)]. However, calculation of 1D junction loss factors is slow and labour intensive [[52](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0379711217303028#bib52)] and the required oversimplification of complex geometries at stations could introduce passenger comfort and safety risks [[53](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0379711217303028#bib53)].

The field of building simulation (the study of ventilation and air quality in buildings) use coupled hybrid modelling – these instances involve the coupling of a “multizone model” (a 1D network model) and a field model [[19](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0379711217303028#bib19),[[54]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0379711217303028#bib54), [[55]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0379711217303028#bib55), [[56]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0379711217303028#bib56), [[57]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0379711217303028#bib57), [[58]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0379711217303028#bib58), [[59]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0379711217303028#bib59), [[60]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0379711217303028#bib60)]. In building simulation, the field sub-model is typically used to simulate external wind conditions around the building and not features inside the building.

Wildland fire researchers use coupled hybrid methods, typically called atmosphere-fire coupling, to examine the interaction of wildfire and atmospheric systems [[23](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0379711217303028#bib23),[[61]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0379711217303028#bib61), [[62]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0379711217303028#bib62), [[63]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0379711217303028#bib63)]. Studies couple a field model (used to simulate mass and enthalpy flow in the atmosphere above a wildfire) and an empirical 2D fire spread model. The fire spread model provides a source of enthalpy to the atmosphere field model which then models large scale atmospheric flow and turbulence with a grid cell size of typically 20–100 m.

3. Coupled hybrid modelling in fire safety engineering
------------------------------------------------------

In fire safety engineering, coupled hybrid modelling can be broken into three categories based on the selection of sub-models. The categories are coupled field-zone, field-network and zone-network hybrid models. The following sub-sections provide a literature review for each category in turn.

### 3.1. Coupled field-zone hybrid models

The earliest of the coupled hybrid model categories to emerge. These models are used to examine building and ship fires. These coupled hybrid models simulate the fire, the enclosure of fire origin and proximal enclosures in the field sub-domain and simulate medium to far field spaces in the zone sub-model. Refer to [Fig. 4](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0379711217303028#fig4).

![Image 14: Fig. 4](https://ars.els-cdn.com/content/image/1-s2.0-S0379711217303028-egi10KJRPDK9NB.jpg)
1.   [Download: Download high-res image (134KB)](https://ars.els-cdn.com/content/image/1-s2.0-S0379711217303028-egi10KJRPDK9NB_lrg.jpg "Download high-res image (134KB)")
2.   [Download: Download full-size image](https://ars.els-cdn.com/content/image/1-s2.0-S0379711217303028-egi10KJRPDK9NB.jpg "Download full-size image")

Fig. 4. Coupled field-zone hybrid model schematic.

Xu et al. [[64](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0379711217303028#bib64)] developed a coupled field-zone hybrid model and documented the results of a numerical demonstration case on a single storey, multi-room building. The field sub-model was 2D and was coupled to a bespoke zone sub-model. No validation of the coupled hybrid model was presented. Wang et al. [[65](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0379711217303028#bib65)] later extended the field sub-model to enable the consideration of 3D cases – the article is not scientifically thorough and presents a short summary of the extended model with no verification or validation.

Fan et al. [[66](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0379711217303028#bib66)], from State Key Laboratory of Fire Science of China, presented a field-zone hybrid method, coupling proprietary unnamed sub-models to create a new model called F-Z model. The field model used k-ε turbulence modelling. In the field sub-domain, the hybrid interface is a zero gradient [Neumann boundary condition](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/neumann-boundary-condition "Learn more about Neumann boundary condition from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") for all variables except the [perpendicular velocity](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/perpendicular-velocity "Learn more about perpendicular velocity from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") component which is output based upon mass conservation. In the zone sub-domain, the hybrid interface is a mass and enthalpy flux boundary condition – computed using relevant summations of variables (velocity, temperature, specific heat, species concentration and density) taken from the adjacent field sub-model grid cells. Heat losses to the bounding construction are ignored.

The authors validated the F-Z Model against medium-scale fire test data of a two-room arrangement with generally good agreement. The hybrid model over-predicts the peak gas temperatures in both the fire room and the connected room by approximately 3–10 °C. The author states that this may be due to the omission of heat loss to the bounding walls. The validation case [heat source](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/earth-and-planetary-sciences/actuator "Learn more about heat source from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") was an electric heater; this limits the conclusions which can be drawn as phenomena such as changes to density and pressure due to mass flux from a fire, soot disposition and spatial variability of burning, due to low equivalence ratio, are neglected.

Fan & Wang [[67](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0379711217303028#bib67)] further developed the F-Z Model, describing an enhanced coupled field-zone-network hybrid model called FZN Ver. 3. Fan et al. [[66](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0379711217303028#bib66)] and Fan & Wang [[67](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0379711217303028#bib67)] contain disagreement in the model descriptions, with the latter publication describing the original F-Z Model as including a network sub-model. We presume that an unpublished version 2 of the FZN model introduced a network sub-model. The authors replaced the network sub-model with a single control volume zone sub-model and altered the zone sub-model pressure modification method. The former change is self-explanatory and the authors state that this optimises the code. There is no discussion of the effect of removing node connections, and hence [axial velocity](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/axial-velocity "Learn more about axial velocity from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), from the network sub-model. The second change is that the zone sub-model of FZN Ver. 3 computes pressure modifications using energy and volume conservation – in contrast to the original model which used mass conservation. The authors state that this method converges faster and more reliably. The article describes the original method as unstable as compartment mass is an implicit parameter, whereas volume is constant and therefore less likely to lead to divergence. This is in contrast to CFAST 6's solution, which is based upon [conservation of mass](https://www.scienced

enriched_points.expanded_content: The project focuses on developing advanced fire safety engineering tools, enhancing building resilience against fire through collaborative research and innovative design methodologies.
Key stakeholders include academic researchers, engineering firms, and regulatory bodies, fostering a collaborative environment for knowledge exchange and best practices in fire safety.
The project yielded significant advancements in fire-resistant design methodologies, resulting in improvements in building codes and enhanced public safety measures.
Future research will focus on integrating emerging technologies like IoT and predictive analytics to further improve fire safety standards and resilience in urban infrastructure.
